source.title: Storage Tank Fire at Intercontinental Terminals Company, LLC (ITC) Terminal

event.costs: $150 million+ property damage; 470,000–523,000 barrels released to waterways
actions_taken: ITC Emergency Response Team (ERT) deployed to tank farm
Plant-wide alarm activated and all-call announcement made
Channel Industries Mutual Aid (CIMA) notified and resources deployed
Unified Command established with ITC, CIMA, HCFMO, Harris County OEM, EPA, USCG, and TCEQ
Water and foam applied in defensive firefighting mode
Third-party emergency response provider (US Fire Pump) contracted
Shelter-in-place orders issued for City of Deer Park
Air monitoring conducted by ITC contractor CTEH and regulatory agencies
Houston Ship Channel closed for seven miles
Vacuum trucks and boom deployed for spill containment and cleanup
Product removal from compromised tanks commenced March 21, 2019
All 15 tanks deconstructed and cleaned by July 29, 2019
event.summary: On March 17, 2019, a large fire erupted at the ITC bulk liquid storage terminal in Deer Park, Texas. The mechanical seal on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump failed, releasing butane-enriched naphtha product for approximately 30 minutes before ignition. The fire spread to all 15 tanks in the First & Second 80's tank farm over three days. Contributing factors included absence of flammable gas detection, absence of remotely operated emergency isolation valves, inadequate tank farm design (spacing, subdivisions, drainage), and lack of a formal mechanical integrity program. The secondary containment wall failed on March 22, 2019, releasing an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of hydrocarbon products, firefighting foam, and contaminated water into Tucker Bayou and the Houston Ship Channel. No fatalities or injuries occurred, but the community experienced multiple shelter-in-place orders and school closures. Property damage exceeded $150 million.
recommendations: 2019-01-I-TX-R1: ITC to develop and implement a PSM system for all atmospheric storage tanks and associated equipment in highly hazardous chemical service at ITC Deer Park terminal.
2019-01-I-TX-R2: ITC to develop and implement a condition monitoring program for all pumps in highly hazardous chemical service, with alarms triggered when control limits are exceeded.
2019-01-I-TX-R3: ITC to install flammable gas detection systems with alarm functions in product storage and transfer areas; develop response plan and operator training.

2019-01-I-TX-R4: ITC to install remotely operated emergency isolation valves configured to Fail-Closed for all atmospheric storage tanks containing highly hazardous chemicals or NFPA-3 or higher flammability rated liquids.

2019-01-I-TX-R5: ITC to evaluate design of all new and existing tank farms against API STD 2610 and NFPA 30 (2021 edition), addressing tank spacing, subdivisions, process equipment placement, and drainage/containment wall adequacy.
2019-01-I-TX-R6: API to update API STD 2610 to include flammable gas detection systems in the leak detection section.
2019-01-I-TX-R7: OSHA to eliminate the atmospheric storage tank exemption from the PSM standard (supersedes 2001-05-I-DE-R1 and 2010-02-PR-R4).
2019-01-I-TX-R8: EPA to modify 40 C.F.R. § 68.115(b)(2)(i) to expand RMP rule coverage to all flammable liquids with NFPA-3 or higher flammability rating (supersedes 2010-02-I-PR-R1).

hazards.name: Large inventory of flammable butane-enriched naphtha in atmospheric storage tank
Rotating pump equipment (circulation pump) handling flammable liquid under pressure

threats.name: Pump mechanical seal failure due to outboard bearing failure and vibration
Absence of formal mechanical integrity program for pump in highly hazardous chemical service
Inadequate tank farm design (spacing, subdivisions, drainage, pump placement inside containment)

consequences.name: Fire engulfing Tank 80-8 piping manifold and spreading to all 15 tanks in tank farm
Destruction of fifteen 80,000-barrel atmospheric storage tanks and contents
Secondary containment wall failure and release of 470,000–523,000 barrels to waterways
Community impact: shelter-in-place orders, school closures, highway closure, benzene air quality concerns
Environmental contamination of Tucker Bayou, Buffalo Bayou, and Houston Ship Channel

controls.name: Pump Mechanical Integrity Program
controls.barrier_role: Prevent pump seal failure through maintenance procedures, training, preventative maintenance, and quality assurance for pumps in highly hazardous chemical service
controls.lod_basis: Primary prevention barrier to prevent equipment failure leading to loss of containment

supporting_text: ITC did not have a formal mechanical integrity procedure in place that defined requirements for maintaining the mechanical integrity of Tank 80-8 and its associated equipment, including the Tank 80-8 circulation pump.
ITC did not have a written pump rebuild or replacement procedure in place that referenced the pump manufacturer's recommended instructions.
ITC did not provide maintenance personnel with formal, documented training related to pump maintenance and repair work and did not have a formal program in place to assess their competency.

ITC did not have a formal Preventative Maintenance (PM) program in place for the Tank 80-8 circulation pump that included routine maintenance and inspection activities recommended by the pump manufacturer.

The ITC Deer Park terminal's management systems lacked essential mechanical integrity program items, such as maintenance procedures, training for pump replacements and rebuilds, and routine preventative maintenance activities as recommended by the pump manufacturer.

controls.name: Vibration Condition Monitoring on Circulation Pump
controls.barrier_role: Detect abnormal pump vibration indicative of bearing degradation before catastrophic failure and release
controls.lod_basis: Early warning detection to allow pump shutdown before seal failure

supporting_text: There was no vibration monitoring system installed on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump nor was there an LEL gas detection system in the area to alert personnel of the ongoing release.

ITC did not retrofit the Tank 80-8 circulation pump with vibration monitoring equipment, which resulted in pump vibration going undetected and allowed the outboard bearing to continue to degrade to failure.

Had ITC installed vibration monitoring equipment on the Tank 80-8 circulation pump, excessive vibration likely would have triggered an alarm, and ITC operators could have shut down the pump before the bearing failed.

controls.name: DCS Alarm Setpoints for Pump Discharge Pressure and Tank Volume
controls.barrier_role: Alert CCR operators to abnormal operating conditions (pressure, flow, tank level) indicative of a release
controls.lod_basis: Process control alarm system as detection layer for abnormal conditions

supporting_text: The unanticipated changes in the computer control system data did not trigger any alarms in the central control room (CCR) since the changes did not reach any applicable alarm setpoints.
The Hi and Lo alarm setpoints for the Tank 80-8 pump discharge pressure were 130 and 0 psi, respectively.

The CCR operator on duty was not actively monitoring Tank 80-8 because no loading or unloading activity was ongoing at the time. As a result, the CCR operator did not identify the ongoing reduction in tank volume and took no measures to secure the butane-enriched naphtha product release.

controls.name: Flammable Gas Detection System (LEL Monitors)
controls.barrier_role: Detect flammable vapors from loss of containment and alert personnel before ignition
controls.lod_basis: Early warning detection to enable personnel response before ignition of flammable vapors

supporting_text: Tank 80-8 was not equipped with a flammable gas detection system to warn personnel of a hazardous atmosphere resulting from loss of containment from the tank or its associated equipment.

In 2014, a hazard review team recommended the addition of flammable gas detection systems near Tank 80-8; however, ITC did not implement this recommendation, and did not document why it was not implemented.

In the absence of a flammable gas detection system, there were no alarms to alert personnel about the initial release of butane-enriched naphtha product around the Tank 80-8 piping manifold. Consequently, the butane-enriched naphtha product continued to release from the failed pump for approximately 30 minutes, completely undetected, before its flammable vapors eventually ignited.
The First & Second 80's tank farm was not equipped with a gas detection system.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) Program
controls.barrier_role: Systematically identify hazards and implement safeguards for Tank 80-8 and associated equipment
controls.lod_basis: Foundational process safety management element to identify and control hazards

supporting_text: Since the OSHA PSM standard and EPA RMP rule did not apply to Tank 80-8, ITC was not required to formally perform a PHA on this system at least once every five years. According to available documentation, Tank 80-8 did not have an initial PHA and was not on a recurring PHA cycle.
ITC did not apply other key elements, such as Mechanical Integrity and Process Hazard Analysis, to atmospheric storage tanks in highly hazardous chemical service.

The project team recommended that ITC 'consider projects for LEL meters/deluge at pumps'; however, ITC ultimately decided not to install an LEL detector or deluge system at the Tank 80-8 circulation pump and did not document a justification for not implementing this safety recommendation.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) Process
controls.barrier_role: Evaluate and document safety implications of process changes such as butane injection system addition
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to ensure hazards from process changes are identified and mitigated

supporting_text: Although the OSHA PSM standard and EPA RMP rule did not apply to Tank 80-8, ITC used its management of change (MOC) process to document the Tank 80-8 Truck Butane Injection System project.

Although ITC applied some PSM elements across its entire Deer Park terminal, such as MOC and incident investigation, the company did not apply other key elements of a comprehensive process safety management system to atmospheric storage tanks in highly hazardous chemical service.

controls.name: Remotely Operated Emergency Isolation Valves (ROEIVs)
controls.barrier_role: Remotely isolate tank contents from piping manifold to stop release and limit fuel supply to fire
controls.lod_basis: First mitigation barrier to stop or limit release after loss of containment event

supporting_text: Tank 80-8 was not equipped with a remotely operated emergency isolation valve (ROEIV) therefore, the Unified Command discussed trying to manually isolate the main valves on the tank. However, because flames engulfed the entire Tank 80-8 piping manifold, so the area could not be safely accessed, allowing the continued release of butane-enriched naphtha product from Tank 80-8.

On the day of the incident, the large volume of butane-enriched naphtha product contained in Tank 80-8 could not be remotely or automatically isolated, and it continued to release, via the failed pump, fueling the fire that continued to intensify around the tank.

Had remotely operated emergency isolation valves (ROEIVs) configured to 'Fail-Closed' been installed on Tank 80-8 and the other fourteen tanks in First & Second 80's tank farm, the tanks could have been secured without the need for personnel to enter the tank farm after power was lost to all motor operated valves (MOVs), and the quantity of the materials released could have been reduced.

ITC did not equip these valves with fusible links, programming logic, or other protective measures to help ensure that these valves would automatically close in the event of a power outage, fire, or other event ('Fail-Closed').

controls.name: Motor Operated Valves (MOVs) on Storage Tanks
controls.barrier_role: Provide remote valve closure capability to isolate tank contents during emergency
controls.lod_basis: Partial mitigation barrier; present on 12 of 15 tanks but not fail-safe configured

supporting_text: ITC had equipped twelve of the fifteen (15) 80,000-barrel storage tanks located in the First & Second 80's tank farm with MOVs; however, ITC did not equip these valves with fusible links, programming logic, or other protective measures to help ensure that these valves would automatically close in the event of a power outage, fire, or other event ('Fail-Closed').

When the flammable butane-enriched naphtha ignited, the resulting fire caused a power outage to the controls communication system almost immediately after ignition, which rendered all the First & Second 80's tank farm MOVs inoperable and incapable of providing remote isolation.

controls.name: Fixed Fire Monitor System (Interior and Perimeter)
controls.barrier_role: Apply water or foam to control fire and cool adjacent tanks to prevent fire spread
controls.lod_basis: Active fire suppression to limit fire escalation and protect adjacent tanks

supporting_text: The first responding member of the ERT entered the tank farm near Tank 80-12 and attempted to activate the fixed fire monitor located in the center of Tanks 80-8, 80-9, 80-11, and 80-12. However, the fire monitor was engulfed in flames, and he was unable to access or activate it.
The acting shift manager described the flow of water coming out of this monitor as a 'trickle' not capable of reaching the fire.
The positions of these fixed fire monitors did not allow for them to be aimed directly at the fire engulfing the Tank 80-8 piping manifold, as they were not designed to reach that area.

During the March 2019 incident, emergency responders were unable to access or activate the fixed fire monitor that was designed to reach Tank 80-8, as it was engulfed in flames from the fire in the Tank 80-8 piping manifold.

controls.name: Fixed Foam System on Tank 80-8
controls.barrier_role: Inject foam into tank to suppress flammable vapors and extinguish tank fire
controls.lod_basis: Active fire suppression system to control tank fire

supporting_text: Tank 80-8 was equipped with a fixed foam system that injected foam into the tank to cover the flammable liquid, to suppress the flammable vapors and cut off oxygen supply necessary to support combustion. The fixed foam system on Tank 80-8 was a manually activated system, with supply coming from fire trucks. The hose connection point was located at the Second 80's piping manifold by Tanks 80-9 and 80-12, which was inundated with fire. This made the area inaccessible to responders.
Based on these two significant safety concerns, the Unified Command decided not to activate Tank 80-8's fixed foam system.

controls.name: Secondary Containment Wall (Dike)
controls.barrier_role: Contain released hydrocarbon products within tank farm boundary to prevent environmental release
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defense to prevent environmental release of spilled materials

supporting_text: On Friday, March 22, 2019, at around 12:15 p.m., the secondary containment wall surrounding the First & Second 80's tank farm partially collapsed near Tank 80-7. The containment wall displaced laterally due to excessive lateral soil and hydrostatic pressures.

The containment wall failure allowed the mixture of released hydrocarbon products, firefighting foam, and contaminated water previously confined to the tank farm to exit the containment area and enter the ditch running adjacent to Tidal Road.

The Natural Resource Damage Assessment and Restoration Program reported that an estimated 470,000–523,000 barrels of a mixture of fire water, firefighting aqueous film-forming foams, and petrochemical products from the storage tanks were released into Tucker Bayou and adjacent waters.

controls.name: Tank Farm Design – Subdivisions (Intermediate Dikes/Curbs)
controls.barrier_role: Limit spread of released flammable liquid between tanks within common containment area
controls.lod_basis: Physical barrier to slow fire and liquid spread between tank groups

supporting_text: ITC's First & Second 80's tank farm was not subdivided as was required by NFPA 30.

The drainage as provided segregates the tank farm into groups of three tanks each, while Section 2173(g)(2) would have required the drainage to be provided such that each tank was separated from adjoining tanks.

Additional subdivisions could have slowed the spread of the butane-enriched naphtha product and allowed responders the opportunity to access and activate the fixed fire monitors located within the containment area to fight the fire surrounding the Tank 80-8 piping manifold before it spread to adjacent tanks.

controls.name: Tank Farm Design – Tank Spacing
controls.barrier_role: Provide sufficient separation between tanks to prevent fire spread by radiant heat
controls.lod_basis: Physical separation to limit fire escalation between adjacent tanks

supporting_text: Although the First & Second 80's tank farm was designed in accordance with applicable NFPA 30 tank farm spacing requirements in place at the time the tank farm was constructed, the NFPA 30 recommendation for additional spacing for compacted tank farm layouts was not included in the design.
ITC spaced the 15 tanks in three rows of five roughly 36 feet from one another, in accordance with the minimum required shell-to-shell spacing distance specified in NFPA 30.
The results of the study indicate that the coupled impact of a tank fire and a liquid pool fire may need to be considered in evaluating the minimum safe separation distance of future installations.

controls.name: Emergency Response Team (ERT) and Unified Command
controls.barrier_role: Coordinate firefighting and emergency response to control fire and protect life safety
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response as recovery barrier to limit consequences after fire ignition

supporting_text: Members of the ERT reported to the fire house and prepared to fight the fire.

A formal Unified Command was established to oversee the coordinated response. The Unified Command included representatives from ITC, CIMA, the Harris County Fire Marshal's Office (HCFMO), the Harris County Office of Emergency Management, the EPA, the U.S. Coast Guard (USCG), and TCEQ.
Emergency responders continued to fight the fire in defensive mode throughout the evening, applying water and foam to prevent the fire from spreading further.
The fire at the ITC Deer Park terminal was extinguished by approximately 3:00 a.m. on Wednesday, March 20, 2019.

controls.name: OSHA PSM Standard and EPA RMP Rule Coverage
controls.barrier_role: Regulatory framework requiring formal process safety management including mechanical integrity, PHA, and MOC for covered processes
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement as foundational driver for implementing safety management systems

supporting_text: ITC did not apply a formal process safety management program to Tank 80-8 because neither the OSHA PSM standard nor the EPA RMP rule applied to Tank 80-8 and its associated equipment.

Tank 80-8 was not covered by the OSHA PSM standard due to the atmospheric storage tank exemption in the standard, and the EPA RMP rule did not apply due to the flammability rating exemption in the rule for the butane-enriched naphtha mixture.

The CSB determined that because of the atmospheric storage tank exemption contained in the OSHA PSM standard and the flammability exemption contained in the EPA RMP rule, ITC was not required to develop and implement a formal PSM program for Tank 80-8 and its associated equipment.

controls.name: Small Concrete Containment Area Around Tank 80-8 Pump
controls.barrier_role: Capture minor leaks and drips from pump and valves and direct to wastewater treatment
controls.lod_basis: Local containment to capture small releases before they reach common containment area

supporting_text: ITC had 'small concrete containment area[s]' immediately adjacent to the majority of the tanks at its facility, including Tank 80-8. ITC's Spill Prevention Control and Countermeasure Plan (SPCC) stated that these concrete containment areas were designed to provide local containment and drainage capabilities for pumps and valves associated with the tanks.
On the day of the incident, the butane-enriched naphtha product released from Tank 80-8 overflowed this small containment area and entered the common containment area of the tank farm.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
